item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm.

Under no circumstances are Foundation personnel to use information gleaned from archival SCP-3162 entries to guide or influence their interpersonal relationships.

As phrased, compliance after exposure to archival entries seems almost literally impossible (the ready availability of amnestics in SCP-world is the only reason for "almost"). Perhaps this could be rephrased to indicate (a) limitations on accessing archival entries, or (b) precisely what personnel should do if they do encounter information that would influence their interpersonal relationships.

SCP-3162 posits that the above-mentioned researchers would be of best use to the Foundation, and society at large, as feed for one of the many carnivorous SCPs housed in Site 19.

lol'ed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: His divorce and spending Christmas alone makes me so sad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the almost over the top clinical tone adopted during this skip, particularly during the last two entries, upon which it becomes apparent that it's not simply a short biography of the person, but also several other facts about them that are relevant to the situation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[[module ListUsers users="."]]
This should have ended with a log about %%title%%. That guy should be contained. [[/module]]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wonderful. Really wonderful idea.
To be honest i regret that those junior doctors acted so childish upon discovering private information thanks to anomalous effects of unknown SCP.
Pity that it was not the D class personnel who came up with an memetic/cognitohazard idea… Would be a nice finisher to the careers of the doctors trio.

Also i missed the reason why Issac marriage ended. Was it because his wife discovered the affair with Mickie?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I rolled my eyes at "entity what lives in a computer", but it got better and better with every archived entry. The last line is the perfect end to this mini-character arc.

Definitely one of my new favorites in Series IV. Phenomenal stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had thought that this wasn't quite ready in the last draft I reviewed, but I think the few extra details have made the difference - especially by tying the D Class log in more closely to the rest of the narrative, and by switching so it wasn't the ending. Pleased to see it doing well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

A clever SCP idea and compelling, if humorous, emotional connection. Evidently the SCP focuses on the parts of the subject's life that are most embarrassing/controversial.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article deserves more attention, even as a borderline joke article. Relationship drama is to be expected within the Foundation, of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This works very well. It's a l m o s t a -J, but works as a mainlist. The story told was easy enough to follow while being intriguingly told.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, this is a rather clever submission! That D-Class entry sold me on an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I have ideas for a few other SCPs that I hope to have submitted in the next few weeks. I like telling stories within the constraints of an SCP entry, rather than just writing an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is quite excellent for a first skip. I always love it when people are able to bring examples of genre fiction that usually don’t do well in this format into an article. Ie 1875’s jump scare, 3999’s stream of consciousness, or 1730’s novella length storyline. This one succeeds in bringing office romance and even romcom style plotting into an SCP, and in such a way that I didn’t feel skeptical at its inclusion or presentation. Well done, and I look forward to seeing your future works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought it was a little basic.
Until the last test, where it shows it can into your personal thoughts, which is really creepy

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked the concept of this SCP and the humorous articles written by those under its effect. However, I feel as if the usage of "blocked-out" data in the articles is over-excessive to the point that they are somewhat difficult to read.
